title: dark-mem: persistent memory unit for dark-agents-v2 (v1)

intent.what: Extract dark-mem from dark-research-mcp into a standalone Go module that exposes a library API AND an MCP server. The library has zero MCP dependency; the binary wires it to MCP. Provide dual-driver persistence (SQLite + Postgres) with a single Store interface. Implement the six operational invariants from the constitution. Expose ~50 tools organized by lifecycle: session, research, vibeflow, ssd, constitution, mods, economy, observability, admin.
intent.why: Three forces converge: (1) SQLite's single-writer mutex is the operational ceiling under multi-agent load — Postgres with proper connection pooling removes it; (2) dark-mem currently lives as `internal/mem/` inside dark-research-mcp, which couples memory to the OSINT router and makes it impossible to evolve independently or share with other agents; (3) the dark-recall v2 plugin auto-prefills recalled research into every LLM turn, and the existing schema does not distinguish cross-session reads from per-session reads — engineering decisions baked into the unit's contract (not a CVE, an architecture gap). The standalone unit lets dark-research-mcp shrink to a pure router, lets other agents import dark-mem, and centralizes the defensive invariants in one audited codebase.

must_have: go.mod declares module github.com/dark-agents/dark-mem with NO mcp-go dependency
internal/store.Store interface exists; both internal/store/sqlite and internal/store/postgres implement it
go test ./tests/dual_driver/... passes against BOTH drivers with identical assertions
dark-mem-mcp binary starts, registers all tools, and survives 1000-call stress test
dark-mem-cli binary supports: migrate, vacuum, schema-status, set-driver
Write audit row emitted for every Save* call (verify in tests/audit_test.go)
Canary check rejects Save* whose payload contains the active canary (verify in tests/canary_test.go)
Per-session Recall filter returns correct subset (verify in tests/session_scope_test.go)
Constitution watchdog raises on SHA256 mismatch (verify in tests/watchdog_test.go)
All 6 invariants from the constitution are exercised by at least one test
should_have: Migrations v1–v6 applied cleanly to a fresh dark.db
Migrations v1–v6 applied cleanly to a fresh Postgres database
Existing dark.db (v3 schema) migrates forward to v6 without data loss
Performance: P50 SaveRun latency < 5ms on SQLite, < 10ms on Postgres (LAN)
Performance: P50 Recall latency < 10ms on SQLite with 10k rows, < 30ms on Postgres with 100k rows
dark-research-mcp imports the library and replaces internal/mem/
could_have: Tool: dark_mem_memory_state returns the LLM-visible memory footprint
Vector recall via pgvector extension (Postgres-only, optional)

from_sqlite_only: 1. Build dark-mem with both drivers (sqlite + postgres)
2. Run dark-mem-cli migrate against existing SQLite dark.db — applies v4-v6
3. Verify write_audit table populated for every legacy write (backfill or skip)
4. Swap DARK_DB_DSN to Postgres; run dark-mem-cli migrate — applies v1-v6 fresh
5. Cut traffic over via DARK_DB_DRIVER env var in opencode.jsonc
from_dark_research_mcp: 1. dark-research-mcp replaces internal/mem/ with import github.com/dark-agents/dark-mem
3. dark-research-mcp v0.x deprecation notice; v1.0 requires dark-mem as dependency
4. opencode.jsonc adds the dark-mem MCP server alongside dark-research-mcp

evidence_required: go test ./... — passes on both drivers
go build ./... — produces dark-mem-mcp.exe + dark-mem-cli.exe + dark-mem-inspect.exe
End-to-end: dark-mem-mcp starts, registers 52 tools, accepts 1000 calls without deadlock
Drift verdict: dark_ssd_drift_judge applied to the generated artifacts vs this spec
